The surgeon completes the separation of the specimen from the surrounding tissue, indicating the end of the excision process.

The surgeon selects a V-lock suture to close the defect in the sigmoid colon.
There are a lot of ways of dealing with these polyps.
Once you have chosen to do a combined procedure, you absolutely could do this in the laparoscopic fashion like you see me doing here.
We could have chosen to do a robotic.
This case took less than an hour.
I didn't think it was worth the setup of the robot for this, although the suturing certainly could have been easier.
But overall, I think that this type of procedure is very beneficial to a patient because they have complete knowledge that the polyp is dealt with.
This was done as an outpatient procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the preservation of the sigmoid colon during the colectomy procedure.

you know, really minimal morbidity to a procedure like this.
Obviously the concern with closing defects like this on the bowel wall is whether or not you will narrow the lumen.
The surgeon closes the defect on the bowel wall transversely, ensuring the lumen is not narrowed.

The surgeon inserts a needle through a five millimeter trocar, causing a slight bend at the needle tip, but continues suturing the bowel wall without any impact on the procedure.
The surgeon imbricates the bowel wall using an over-and-over suture technique, similar to the closure of a common channel in an intracorporeal anastomosis.
I maintain that I believe that all of us as minimally invasive surgeons should be very comfortable with intracorporeal suturing.
This type of procedure certainly wouldn't be possible if the surgeon was not comfortable doing this.
I think that it's well within the skill set for all of us to be able to do it.
You could make the argument that you could have just put a stapler across this.

Lots of different ways.
I think that the intracorporeal suturing that you're seeing here is a really nice way, and that's why I chose it.
The surgeon completes the closure of the surgical site in two layers, using a silk stitch as a stay suture positioned laterally, and ties the stitch to secure it.
